mnemonics.title: RULO = Four Requirements
mnemonics.content: Random (truly random key), Unique length (key >= message length), Lone use (key used only ONCE), Only between sender/receiver (kept secret). RULO = the four pillars of OTP security.

mnemonics.title: XOR = eXclusive OR = Self-Inverse
mnemonics.content: Encrypt: C = P XOR K. Decrypt: P = C XOR K. Same operation both ways! XOR is self-inverse: applying XOR twice with the same key returns the original. Simple, fast, and mathematically perfect.

mnemonics.title: Shannon Says Perfect
mnemonics.content: Claude Shannon (1949) proved OTP = Perfect Secrecy. For any ciphertext, ALL possible plaintexts are EQUALLY likely. Even infinite computing power reveals nothing. Shannon + OTP = mathematically unbreakable.

mnemonics.title: Reuse = Ruin
mnemonics.content: If same key K encrypts M1 and M2: C1 XOR C2 = M1 XOR M2. The key CANCELS OUT! This is exactly what happened in VENONA (Soviets reused OTP keys, US broke their messages). ONE-time means ONE time.

mnemonics.title: GDM = Three Practical Problems
mnemonics.content: Generation (need truly random keys as long as messages), Distribution (must securely deliver massive keys), Management (store, synchronize, destroy used keys). GDM makes OTP impractical despite being theoretically perfect.

keyPoints: The OTP is the only cipher with mathematically proven perfect secrecy (Shannon, 1949).
Four requirements: truly random key, key length >= plaintext length, single use, kept secret.
Uses XOR for encryption (C = P XOR K) and decryption (P = C XOR K).
Key reuse destroys security: C1 XOR C2 = M1 XOR M2 eliminates the key entirely.
Impractical for most applications due to key generation, distribution, and management challenges.
The OTP is a special case of the Vigenere cipher with a non-repeating random key.
commonMistakes: Confusing 'computationally secure' (hard to break with current technology) with 'perfectly secure' (impossible to break even with unlimited resources). Only OTP is perfectly secure.
Assuming pseudorandom keys provide the same security as truly random keys; pseudorandom keys have patterns that can be exploited.
Forgetting that reusing an OTP key even once completely destroys perfect secrecy.
Thinking the OTP is impractical because the algorithm is complex; the algorithm (XOR) is trivially simple -- it is the key management that is the real challenge.
